lesson.menu_title: Roads Not Taken -- Regrets and What Might Have Been
lesson.menu_desc: Talking about unreal pasts and regrets with the third conditional and wish -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 10 -- Roads Not Taken
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Marcelo de Azevedo Souza | Aula 10 -- Roads Not Taken

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: to choose the careful, low-risk option
understanding a situation only after it has happened
a moment of decision between two very different paths
a bold act of trust in an uncertain outcome
a chance you did not take and later wish you had
to doubt a choice you have already made
to use something as fully and well as possible
suddenly, without planning it in advance

reading.rtitle: The Trip I Almost Didn't Take
paras: In hindsight, it is almost funny how close I came to saying no. A friend had invited me on a two-week cycling trip through the mountains of Albania, and every sensible part of me wanted to play it safe. I was too busy, I told myself; the timing was wrong. I very nearly talked myself out of it. If I had listened to that careful voice, I would have missed the best trip of my life.

I remember standing at a kind of fork in the road. On one side was the comfortable plan I already knew. On the other was a leap of faith into something completely uncertain. I had second thoughts right up until the morning of the flight. But I went, almost on a whim, with a bag half-packed and no real plan. If I had not taken that risk, I would never have met the people who became my closest friends on the road.

Now, years later, I look back on it as the moment everything opened up. I do not regret a single sore muscle or rainy night. The truth about regret is strange: we rarely regret the things we did. We regret the missed opportunities, the roads we did not take because we were busy being careful. If only I had learned that lesson sooner. These days, when a door opens, I try to make the most of it.
reading.source: Adapted from a travel-writing essay, The Roads We Choose

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The writer explains how to plan a safe cycling holiday.
The writer almost said no to a trip, took the risk anyway, and learned that we regret the chances we did not take.
The writer argues that adventure travel is dangerous and should be avoided.

items: From the very start, the writer was sure he wanted to go on the trip.
He nearly talked himself out of it and had second thoughts right up until the morning of the flight.
The writer describes the decision as a fork in the road.
He says he remembers standing at a kind of fork in the road, between the comfortable plan and a leap of faith.
Looking back, the writer regrets going on the trip.
He says he does not regret a single sore muscle or rainy night.
The writer believes we mostly regret the chances we did not take.
He says we rarely regret the things we did; we regret the missed opportunities and the roads we did not take.

parts: a cycling trip, because part of him wanted to
taken the risk, he would never have met his closest friends.

gapfill.text: This is the grammar of an UNREAL past. THIRD CONDITIONAL imagines a different past that did not happen: 'If I had taken the risk (but I did or did not), I would have met them.' Form: if + PAST PERFECT (had + past participle), ... would have + past participle. WISH and IF ONLY + PAST PERFECT express regret about the past: 'I wish I had gone', 'If only I had known'. The key is the double step back: had + participle for the condition, would have + participle for the imagined result.

bank.label: Useful language for talking about regrets
items: In hindsight, I should have said yes.
If I had known, I would have gone.
I wish I had started sooner.
If only I hadn't been so careful.
I don't regret it for a second.
It was a real leap of faith.
I nearly talked myself out of it.
Looking back, it was the best decision I ever made.

lf.title: Real or imagined? (blue = the unreal past -- third conditional or wish)
items: on the trip and loved every minute.
to that fear, I would have stayed home.

lf.text: The plain past (went, don't regret) describes what really happened. The blue phrases describe an UNREAL past: 'had listened' (past perfect after if) leads to 'would have stayed', and 'wish I had + participle' expresses a regret. That double step back in time -- had + participle -- is the heart of the third conditional and of wish.

items: Tell the story of a time you almost said no to something but did it anyway. Use 'If I hadn't ... , I would have ...'.
Talk about a missed opportunity you still think about. Use 'I wish I had ...' or 'If only I had ...'.
A younger person is afraid to take a risk and wants to play it safe. Give them honest advice, with one example from your own life.
Look back on a decision that changed your life. Set the scene, then say what would have happened if you had chosen the other road.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = talked himself out of, 2 = play it safe, 3 = fork in the road, 4 = second thoughts, 5 = on a whim, 6 = had not.
Model (third conditional): 'If I hadn't taken that trip, I would never have met my closest friends.'
Model (wish / regret): 'I wish I had started travelling in my twenties. If only I hadn't been so careful for so long.'
Structure: third conditional = if + past perfect (had + participle), ... would have + past participle -- an unreal, imagined past. wish / if only + past perfect = a regret about the past.
answerkey.note: Never say 'If I would have known'. The if-part uses the past perfect: 'If I had known, I would have gone'. And after wish, use had + participle for the past: 'I wish I had gone' (not 'I wish I went' for a past regret).

listenings.text: Marcelo, it's Greta. I have been thinking about our conversation on the trail, about the roads not taken. You know, when I was thirty, I was offered a job in New Zealand, and I turned it down. I was scared, I wanted to play it safe. For years I told myself it had been the sensible choice. But in hindsight, I wish I had gone. If I had said yes, my whole life would have been completely different. I am not saying it would have been better, just different. What I have learned is this: I do not regret the risks I took. I only regret the one I did not. So when you get to that trip to Norway in November, promise me you will make the most of every single day. Take the harder road. You will never regret it. Talk soon.

Welcome back to the show. Today, the science of regret, and why it does not work the way you think. Here is the surprising part. When researchers ask older people what they regret most, they almost never mention the risks that went wrong. They mention the chances they did not take. The trip they talked themselves out of. The person they never called. The leap of faith they were too afraid to make. We imagine that playing it safe protects us, but in the long run, it is the missed opportunities that stay with us. So here is the takeaway. The next time you are standing at a fork in the road, and one path is safe and one is a little frightening, remember this. In twenty years, you are far more likely to regret the road you did not take than the one you did. If only more people understood that a little sooner.
